title: Running a multi-bucket bokashi rotation for a family household
subtitle: Using two or three bokashi buckets in rotation to handle a full family food waste stream continuously
excerpt: A single bokashi bucket fills in 2-3 weeks for a family of four and takes a further 2 weeks to mature. Without a second bucket, the household has nowhere to put food waste during maturation. A two-bucket rotation solves this; a three-bucket rotation gives flexibility for large cooking events and a reliable supply of mature bokashi for garden burial or compost input. This covers the rotation logic, output management, and what to do with the liquid byproduct.
sourceNotes: Bokashi Living UK technical documentation and rotation guides. Garden Organic guidance on bokashi as compost input. RHS advice on soil amendment with acidic bokashi material. Zero Waste Scotland food waste diversion case studies.

glossaryTerms.definition: An anaerobic fermentation system for kitchen waste using inoculated bran to preserve food scraps rather than decompose them. The pickled material must be buried or added to a compost heap to complete decomposition.

content.text: Bokashi

fermentation works as a closed anaerobic process: a bucket fills over 2-3 weeks, then requires a further 2 weeks sealed before the contents are ready for burial or compost addition. For a household generating food waste continuously, one bucket means a gap. Two buckets eliminate the gap; a third builds in margin.
The two-bucket rotation
Bucket A is active: all food waste goes in, sprinkled with bokashi bran after each addition. Press contents down to expel air pockets before sealing the lid each time.
When bucket A is full, seal it completely and start bucket B for active use. Bucket A sits undisturbed in a cool spot for 14 days.
After 14 days, bucket A is ready. Bury or apply the contents, then clean the bucket ready to become the new active bucket when B fills.
The three-bucket rotation

Three buckets are useful if the household generates large volumes during preserving season or if the garden burial schedule is not regular. The third bucket acts as a matured buffer: once ready, it waits until you have time to dig it in.
Bucket A: active (filling).
Bucket B: sealed and maturing (14 days).
Bucket C: fully matured, waiting for garden burial when time allows.
Disposing of the output
Matured bokashi output is highly acidic (pH 3.5-4.5) and must be neutralised before it contacts plant roots. The two routes:

Garden burial: dig a trench or individual holes 200-300 mm deep, bury the contents, and cover with at least 100 mm of soil. Keep covered soil moist. Within 2-4 weeks the material has largely disappeared, converted by soil microbes into plant-available nutrients. Do not plant immediately into the burial site; the acidity persists for 1-2 weeks.

Compost heap addition: add mature bokashi to an active heap in thin layers (no more than 50 mm at a time), covered by carbon material. Sprinkle garden lime if available to neutralise the acidity. In a hot heap, the bokashi converts within 3-4 weeks. In a cold heap, allow 6-8 weeks.
The bokashi liquid
Bokashi buckets with a tap produce a brownish liquid every few days. This liquid is acidic (pH 3-4) and should not be poured directly on plants undiluted. Uses:
Diluted 1:100 with water: soil drench for established plants.
Undiluted: pour neat down sinks and drains. The microbial community in the liquid digests biofilm and reduces drain odour.

No garden: for households in flats, the community garden, allotment site, or local urban farm will usually accept matured bokashi output. Most sites are happy to take it as a compost activator if offered in sealed buckets.

attrs.symptom: Active bucket smells putrid rather than vinegary
attrs.cause: The bucket was not sealed properly after additions, or insufficient bran was used, allowing aerobic decomposition and pathogens to develop
attrs.fix: A vinegary or pickled smell is correct. A putrid or rotten smell means the batch has failed. Add extra bran, press down hard, and seal firmly. If the smell does not improve within 2 days, discard and restart with the clean bucket.

attrs.symptom: Soil around burial site is not breaking down the bokashi after 4 weeks
attrs.cause: Buried in dry soil, or soil temperature was below 10 degrees C at time of burial
attrs.fix: Water the burial site after covering. In cold weather, burial sites take longer; give it 6-8 weeks. A site that stayed dry may show no decomposition at all; water it and it will restart.
